en.extract:
The banded bay cuckoo or bay-banded cuckoo (Cacomantis sonneratii) is a species of small cuckoo found in the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. Like others in the genus they have  round nostrils. They are usually founded in well-wooded areas mainly in the lower hills. Males sing from exposed branches during the breeding season, which can vary with region. They are distinctive both in their calls as well as plumage with a white eyebrowed appearance and the rufous upperparts with regular dark bands and the whitish underside with fine striations.


Description

The adults are bright rufous or bay on the head and back and are broadly barred with dark brown. The bill is long and slightly curved. A whitish supercilium is distinctive above a dark eye-line. The wing is darker and tail is graduated with a dark brown centre. The tail has a subterminal black band and white tips to the feathers. The sexes are alike. The iris is yellow and the bill is black while the base of the lower mandible is greenish grey. The tarsi are grey. The juvenile is similar but has a pale lower mandible and white fringes to the feathers of the upper body.
The overall length is about 22 cm making it about the same size as the syntopic Cacomantis merulinus and Cacomantis variolosus. The hepatic forms of those can be similar but supercilium, long beak and barred tail distinguish this species.


Taxonomy and systematics
The species was originally classed in the genus Cuculus by John Latham. The type specimen came from Northeastern India. The species epithet is after the French naturalist and explorer Pierre Sonnerat.
Four subspecies are generally recognized:

the nominate form from India, Nepal, Thailand, Malay Peninsula
C. s. musicus Ljungh, 1804 (from Java, Bali)
C. s. fasciolatus Müller, 1843 (Sumatra)
C. s. waiti Baker, 1919 (Sri Lanka)
Some sources also recognize malayanus (Chasen & Kloss, 1931) from Peninsular Malaysia and schlegeli (Junge, 1948) from Sumatra, Borneo and Palawan (SW Philippines).


Ecology and behaviour
Like many other cuckoos, they are brood-parasitic and hosts recorded include the common iora, red-whiskered bulbul, white-bellied erpornis, scarlet minivet, bulbuls and small babblers (Stachyris spp.). The eggs resemble those of the hosts. The incubation and nesting are not well documented. Fledglings of the host are evicted.
Populations are often migratory or partially migratory. In India, they are found mainly during the monsoons.
They are found in well-wooded forests, mainly in hill areas. Insects are their primary diet. They capture insects by gleaning as well as by aerial sallying.
The breeding season varies widely from region to region. Near Bombay they are known to lay eggs from February to August, Assam from April to August while they seem to sing through much of the year in the Eastern Ghats. In Sri Lanka, young have been seen in June and October while adults sing from January to May in the Malay Peninsula.
The call of this species is distinctive. It is high-pitched four note whistle that has been transcribed as "wee-ti wee-tee" or "smoke-yer-pepper". The frequency starts at 2.4 kHz and each note falls in pitch with the strophe lasting a second.


References


External links

Internet Bird Collection
Calls from Xeno-Canto

[[File:Cacomantis_sonneratii.ogg|thumb|Calls]] | status = LC | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = {{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Cacomantis sonneratii'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22683917A93007354 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22683917A93007354.en |access-date=12 November 2021}} | genus = Cacomantis | species = sonneratii | authority = ([[John Latham (ornithologist)|Latham]], 1790) | range_map = Banded Bay Cuckoo ebird data map.png | range_map_caption = Global range{{leftlegend|#007F00|Year-Round Range|outline=gray}}{{leftlegend|#E0CF01|Summer Range|outline=gray}}{{leftlegend|#0080FF|Winter Range|outline=gray}} | synonyms = ''Penthoceryx sonneratii'' }} The '''banded bay cuckoo''' or '''bay-banded cuckoo''' ('''''Cacomantis sonneratii''''') is a species of small [[cuckoo]] found in the [[Indian subcontinent]] and [[Southeast Asia]]. Like others in the genus they have round nostrils. They are usually founded in well-wooded areas mainly in the lower hills. Males sing from exposed branches during the breeding season, which can vary with region. They are distinctive both in their calls as well as plumage with a white eyebrowed appearance and the rufous upperparts with regular dark bands and the whitish underside with fine striations. ==Description== [[File:CacomantisSonneratii.jpg|thumb|left|Head pattern]] The adults are bright rufous or bay on the head and back and are broadly barred with dark brown. The bill is long and slightly curved. A whitish supercilium is distinctive above a dark eye-line. The wing is darker and tail is graduated with a dark brown centre. The tail has a subterminal black band and white tips to the feathers. The sexes are alike. The iris is yellow and the bill is black while the base of the lower mandible is greenish grey. The tarsi are grey.{{cite book|author=Payne, Robert B. |year=2005| title=The cuckoos |publisher=Oxford University Press| isbn=0-19-850213-3 |pages=430–433}} The juvenile is similar but has a pale lower mandible and white fringes to the feathers of the upper body.{{cite book|pages=219–220|url= https://archive.org/stream/faunaofbritishin03oate#page/219/mode/1up/|title= The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds| volume=3| year=1895| author=Blanford WT|publisher= Taylor and Francis, London}}{{cite book|pages=157–160|title= The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds|volume=4| edition=2nd| author=Baker, ECS|url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds4/BakerFBI4#page/n186/mode/1up/ |publisher=Taylor and Francis, London| year=1927}} The overall length is about 22 cm making it about the same size as the syntopic ''[[Cacomantis merulinus]]'' and ''[[Cacomantis variolosus]]''. The hepatic forms of those can be similar but supercilium, long beak and barred tail distinguish this species.{{cite book|author=Rasmussen, PC|author2=JC Anderton|name-list-style=amp|year=2005 |title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide| publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|volume=2|page=227}} ==Taxonomy and systematics== The species was originally classed in the genus ''Cuculus'' by John Latham. The type specimen came from Northeastern India.{{cite book| last= Latham| first= John | year=1790 | title=Index Ornithologicus, Sive Systema Ornithologiae: Complectens Avium Divisionem In Classes, Ordines, Genera, Species, Ipsarumque Varietates | volume=1 | language=Latin | place=London | publisher = Leigh & Sotheby | page=215 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/53510586 }}{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=22 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476474 }} (India, North Cachar Hills) The species epithet is after the French naturalist and explorer [[Pierre Sonnerat]].{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn= 978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=359 }} Four subspecies are generally recognized: * the nominate form from India, Nepal, Thailand, Malay Peninsula * ''C. s. musicus'' [[Sven Ingemar Ljungh|Ljungh]], 1804 (from Java, Bali) * ''C. s. fasciolatus'' Müller, 1843 (Sumatra) * ''C. s. waiti'' [[Edward Charles Stuart Baker|Baker]], 1919 (Sri Lanka){{cite journal|author=Baker, ECS |year=1919| title= [Notes on the species ''Penthoceryx sonnerati'' and description of a new subspecies (''P.s. waiti'') from Ceylon|journal=Bull. Brit. Orn. Club |volume=39|pages=45–47}} Some sources also recognize ''malayanus'' (Chasen & [[C. Boden Kloss|Kloss]], 1931){{cite journal |author1=F. N. Chasen |author2=C. Boden Kloss |year=1931 |title=Five New Malaysian Birds |journal=Bulletin of the Raffles Museum |volume=5 |url=https://lkcnhm.nus.edu.sg/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/app/uploads/2017/06/05brm082-086.pdf |pages=82–86}} from Peninsular Malaysia and ''schlegeli'' (Junge, 1948) from Sumatra, Borneo and Palawan (SW Philippines). ==Ecology and behaviour== Like many other cuckoos, they are brood-parasitic and hosts recorded include the [[common iora]],{{cite journal|author=Neelakantan, KK |year=1969| title=The Baybanded Cuckoo in North Kerala|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=9|issue=12|page=3| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW9#page/n164/mode/1up/}} [[red-whiskered bulbul]], [[white-bellied erpornis]], [[scarlet minivet]], bulbuls and small babblers (''[[Stachyris]]'' spp.). The eggs resemble those of the hosts.{{cite journal|author=Harrison, CJO |year=1969| title= The identification of the eggs of the smaller Indian Cuckoos| journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.| volume=66| issue=3| pages=478–488|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47961603}}{{cite journal|author=Abdulali, Humayun |year=1943| title=The eggs of the Indian Bay-Banded Cuckoo (''Penthoceryx sonneratii'')|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.| volume=44| issue=1| page=122|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48294665}} The incubation and nesting are not well documented. Fledglings of the host are evicted. Populations are often migratory or partially migratory. In India, they are found mainly during the monsoons.{{cite journal|author=Nair, KN |year=1969| title= Bay Banded Cuckoo|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=9|issue=10|page=7| url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW9#page/n142/mode/1up/}} They are found in well-wooded forests, mainly in hill areas. Insects are their primary diet. They capture insects by [[Gleaning (birds)|gleaning]] as well as by [[Hawking (birds)|aerial sallying]]. The breeding season varies widely from region to region. Near Bombay they are known to lay eggs from February to August, Assam from April to August while they seem to sing through much of the year in the [[Eastern Ghats]]. In Sri Lanka, young have been seen in June and October while adults sing from January to May in the Malay Peninsula.{{cite book|author=Ali, S|author2=S D Ripley|name-list-style=amp| title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|edition=2nd|publisher=Oxford University Press|volume= 3|year= 1981 |pages =215–217 }} The call of this species is distinctive. It is high-pitched four note whistle that has been transcribed as "wee-ti wee-tee" or "smoke-yer-pepper". The frequency starts at 2.4 kHz and each note falls in pitch with the strophe lasting a second. ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Cacomantis sonneratii}} * [http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/banded-bay-cuckoo-cacomantis-sonneratii Internet Bird Collection] * [http://www.xeno-canto.org/asia/browse.php?query=Cacomantis+sonneratii Calls from Xeno-Canto] {{Taxonbar|from=Q863195}} [[Category:Cacomantis|banded bay cuckoo]] [[Category:Birds of Indomalaya]] [[Category:Birds described in 1790|banded bay cuckoo]]

zh.extract:
栗斑杜鹃（学名：Cacomantis sonneratii）为杜鹃科八聲杜鵑屬的鸟类，俗名波斑杜鹃。分布于自中国以南西抵印度、斯里兰卡、东抵菲律宾、印度尼西亚诸岛以及中国大陆的四川、云南、广西等地，多见于山林或其他密林中的开阔场所、有时到耕地附近的树丛中以及常久停于秃枝顶上。该物种的模式产地在印度。


亚种
栗斑杜鹃指名亚种（学名：Cacomantis sonneratii sonneratii）。分布于印度至中南半岛以及中国大陆的四川、云南、广西等地。该物种的模式产地在印度。


同物異名
Penthoceryx sonneratii
Cuculus sonneratii


参考文献
